facts_disclosed: Shadowing physio at Bristol City FC, loves elite environment

turns.content: ngl ive had a mad couple of weeks and i need to just get my head straight about something
I'm here. What's been going on?

so basically right one of my lecturers at uni put me forward for this shadowing opportunity. ive been going into Bristol City twice a week. like the football club. shadowing the physio department there
That's a serious opportunity. What's the setup like -- are you mostly observing or getting involved?

bit of both tbh. mostly observing the lead physio Craig but he lets me sit in on assessments and sometimes do hands on work under supervision. but bruv the facilities are something else. hydrotherapy pool, anti-gravity treadmill, cryotherapy chambers. every piece of equipment you could want is just THERE. the level of detail in their rehab programs is next level you know. one player had a grade 2 hamstring and the return to play protocol had like 15 stages. every single metric tracked

That kind of precision is something you almost never see outside of elite sport. When you have the resources and the staff ratio to track everything that closely, the rehab outcomes must be significantly better. How's it sitting with you overall?

thats the thing init. because icl i love it. like properly love it. the environment the standards the way everyone is so dialled in on the athletes recovery. Craig was showing me how they use GPS data from training to adjust rehab loads in real time and i was like wow this is what physio looks like at its absolute best. i could see myself doing this you know

It sounds like you're genuinely inspired by it. That GPS integration with rehab programming is cutting-edge. And Craig clearly rates you if he's investing time in showing you the detail behind it all. Have you thought about whether this is something you'd seriously pursue?

thats whats doing my head in. because i had a proper clear plan you know. graduate, get into community work, build something meaningful. and i still want that. but then i walk into that facility and see what physio can be when you have unlimited resources and i think man i could do this. Craig said my clinical reasoning is sharp for a student which was mad to hear from someone at that level. hes been talking me through the whole career pathway into professional sport

It's not unusual for your plans to evolve when you're exposed to something new. And having a mentor who rates you in that environment is valuable regardless of which direction you go. The skills you'd develop in elite sport -- the precision, the data-driven approach, the high-stakes decisions -- those are transferable wherever you end up. It doesn't have to be an either/or thing permanently.

yeah thats what im starting to think. like its not a forever decision is it. i could go into elite sport for a few years learn everything i can and then bring that level of detail into community settings where they normally dont have it. bring that standard to people who usually just get 6 sessions on the NHS and a leaflet with exercises on it

Exactly. There's no rule that says your career has to follow one straight line. Having experience in an elite environment would actually make you more effective in community work later -- you'd bring a standard of care those settings almost never see. For now, make the most of the shadowing. Learn everything Craig is willing to teach you. The bigger picture will come together.

yeah youre right. im gonna stop overthinking it and just absorb everything while im there. Craig said i can keep coming in until the end of the season which is months of learning. need to balance it with everything else but thats a problem for another day init. safe for letting me talk through it bruv

That's the right mindset. You've got an open door at a professional club with a mentor who rates you -- that's not something you turn away from. Enjoy the experience and trust the bigger picture will sort itself out. Good luck with the rest of the shadowing.
